text: VO2max declines significantly in master endurance runners regardless of gender or age group, at a rate similar to or greater than that expected in sedentary older adults.
population: master endurance runners (men ~54 yr, women ~49 yr)

text: The rate of VO2max decline in male master endurance runners ranges from -1% to -4.6% per year depending on age group.
The rate of VO2max decline in female master endurance runners ranges from -0.5% to -2.4% per year depending on age group.

In male master runners, the greatest loss in VO2max is associated with the greatest loss in lean body mass (-2.8 kg), suggesting that maintaining lean body mass is key to preserving aerobic capacity in aging men.

In female master runners, the greatest loss in VO2max is associated with the greatest reduction in training volume (-24.1 km/week), suggesting that maintaining training volume is critical for preserving aerobic capacity in aging women.
Post-menopausal female master runners who did not use estrogen replacement had the greatest VO2max decline (-9.6 mL/kg/min), independent of age.
Maximal heart rate declines significantly over time in master athletes regardless of gender, and this decline is not influenced by changes in VO2max or training volume.
